title: Last Words. Napoleon and Wellington
content:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ NAPOLEON.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Is it this, then, O world-warrior,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ That, exulting, through the folds
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Of the dark and cloudy barrier
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Thine enfranchised eye beholds?
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Is, when blessed hands relieve thee
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ From the gross and mortal clay,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ This the heaven that should receive thee?
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Tן¿½te dן¿½armן¿½e.ן¿½

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Now the final link is breaking,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Of the fierce, corroding chain,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And the ships, their watch forsaking,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Bid the seas no more detain,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Whither is it, freed and risen,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The pure spirit seeks away,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Quits for what the weary prison?
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Tן¿½te dן¿½armן¿½e.ן¿½

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Doubtless angels, hovering oן¿½er thee
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ In thine exileן¿½s sad abode,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Marshalled even now before thee,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Move upon that chosen road!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Thither they, ere friends have laid thee
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Where sad willows oן¿½er thee play,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Shall already have conveyed thee!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Tן¿½te dן¿½armן¿½e.ן¿½

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Shall great captains, foiled and broken,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Hear from thee on each great day,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ At the crisis, a word spoken
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Word that battles still obey
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Cuirassiers here, here those cannon;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Quick, those squadrons, up-away!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½To the charge, on as one man, on!ן¿½
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Tן¿½te dן¿½armן¿½e.ן¿½

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ (Yes, too true, alas! while, sated
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Of the wars so slow to cease,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Nations, once that scorned and hated,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Would to Wisdom turn, and Peace;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Thy dire impulse still obeying,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Fevered youths, as in the old day,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ In their hearts still find thee saying,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Tן¿½te dן¿½armן¿½e.ן¿½)

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Oh, poor soul! Or do I view thee,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ From earthן¿½s battle-fields withheld,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ In a dream, assembling to thee
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Troops that quell not, nor are quelled,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Breaking airy lines, defeating
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Limbo-kings, and, as to-day,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Idly to all time repeating
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Tן¿½te dן¿½armן¿½e.ן¿½

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ WELLINGTON.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And what the words, that with his failing breath
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Did England hear her aged soldier say?
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ I know not. Yielding tranquilly to death,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ With no proud speech, no boast, he passed away.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Not stirring words, nor gallant deeds alone,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Plain patient work fulfilled that length of life;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Duty, not glory Service, not a throne,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Inspired his effort, set for him the strife.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Therefore just Fortune, with one hasty blow,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Spurning her minion, Gloryן¿½s, Victoryן¿½s lord,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Gave all to him that was content to know,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ In service done its own supreme reward.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The words he said, if haply words there were,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ When full of years and works he passed away.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Most naturally might, methinks, refer
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ To some poor humble business of to-day.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½That humble simple duty of the day
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Perform,ן¿½ he bids; ן¿½ask not if small or great:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Serve in thy post; be faithful, and obey;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Who serves her truly, sometimes saves the State.ן¿½
meaning:
This evocative piece by Arthur Hugh Clough represents a masterful exploration of classic. The lines capture a profound emotional resonance, inviting the reader to reflect on the deeper themes of human experience and artistic expression.

In the broader context of Arthur Hugh Clough's bibliography, these specific lines stand out for their clarity and poignancy. Whether you are searching for inspiration or a moment of quiet contemplation, this work offers a timeless perspective that transcends its original era.

At Linespedia, we have curated this specific poem to ensure it reaches a modern audience. We recommend sharing these lines as a digital poster or using them as a thoughtful caption to connect with others who appreciate the enduring beauty of verse.
